segments.s553-g33: برانوش is the woman's name; جنگی is a postpositive epithet meaning “warlike / warrior” attached to the name. به + قلب اندرون marks the location — به here means “into / in” and قلب اندرون (قلب + اندرون) is a poetic compound meaning the inner heart or interior of the chest.
segments.s553-g34: گرفتار شد is a past passive/resultative construction: گرفتـار (seized/caught/stricken) + شد (auxiliary ‘became/was’), so “was seized/overcome.” با introduces the manner/condition (“with”), followed by دلی (a heart, here a bodily/figurative heart) modified by the adjective phrase پر ز خون = “full of blood” (پر = full (of), ز = poetic “of/from”). The whole phrase describes her condition after the wound.
segments.s553-g35: وزان is a conjunctive/adverbial link “and from that / and from them.” رومیان is the affected group (“the Romans”). کشته شد is the passive past “were killed/ slain”; the numeral سه هزار (three thousand) is the quantity associated with the passive verb (three thousand were killed).
segments.s553-g36: بالتوینه is a proper-name/obscure compound (a place or unit name) and stands before the locative phrase. در = “in”; صف کارزار = “the battle-line/formation” (صف = line/formation, کارزار = the battle). Together they give the place/position where those killed lay or fell.
segments.s553-g37: هزار و دو سیصد is the numeral written in full (one thousand and two hundred = 1,200). The conjunction و simply links parts of the numeral here. گرفتار شد is again the passive/resultative “were captured / were taken.” The clause states the number taken.
segments.s553-g38: دل جنگیان is a genitive phrase meaning “the hearts of the warriors” (دل noun followed by جنگیان in the possessive/genitive role). پر ز تیمار شد is the predicative phrase: پر = “full (of)”, ز = poetic preposition “of/from”, تیمار = “care/concern/treatment”, شد = “became” — so “their hearts became full of care/concern.”
segments.s553-g39: فرستاد is the past transitive “sent / dispatched.” قیصر is the agent (Caesar/the emperor). یکی یادگیر is the direct object: یکی = “a certain / one (person),” یادگیر literally “learner” but here likely an agent sent to learn or gather news (an envoy/agent). Word order places the verb before the agent and object, a regular pattern in classical poetic Persian.
segments.s553-g40: به = “to/unto”; نزدیک here means “near / into the presence of” (به نزدیک = “to the presence of”). شاپور is the personal name; شاه is the title “king”; اردشیر is the royal name. The sequence names the addressee: Shapur, King Ardashir.
segments.s553-g65: به ... اندرون here marks motion or placement into the interior: 'into/within Pars'. The noun order is inward locative + place + noun: 'پارس' (Pars) + 'اندرون' (interior) + 'شارستان' (town), with the adjective 'بلند' (lofty) following the noun as a descriptive modifier.
segments.s553-g66: برآورد is a literary past-tense verb meaning 'he erected/caused to be' (here 'built, raised'). The adjectives 'پاکیزه و سودمند' follow the noun and qualify the town ('clean/neat and useful/profitable'). Word order is noun phrase + verb, typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s553-g67: یکی here is indefinite 'a certain (one)/someone' as subject. 'شارستان کرد' is a light verbal clause: 'کرد' (did/made) with شارستان as the thing made — 'built a town'. 'در سیستان' is the locative complement 'in Sistan'.
segments.s553-g68: 'در آنجای' = 'in that place'; آنجای is an older form of آن جا. 'بسیار' functions as a quantifier before the noun here ('many'), so 'بسیار خرماستان' = 'many date-groves' or 'a place with many date-groves'.
segments.s553-g69: که introduces the relative/explanatory clause. 'یک نیم او' = 'one half of it' with او referring back to the town; the perfect-pluperfect 'کرده بود' ('had made') expresses anterior past, and the agent 'اردشیر' follows the verb (a common narrative order).
segments.s553-g70: 'دگر نیم' = 'the other half'. The name 'شاپور' is given as the agent of that half; 'گرد' here is likely part of an epithet or compound name (or an archaic epithet) paired with 'و دلیر' = 'and brave'. Word order places the agent/name after the object phrase.
segments.s553-g71: 'کهن دژ' is a noun phrase 'the old/ancient fortress'. 'به شهر نشاپور کرد' means 'he made (it) into the city of Nishapur' or 'turned it into Nishapur city', with the verb 'کرد' final as the past action.
segments.s553-g72: که introduces a reported clause 'which/that they say ...'. گویند is the impersonal reporting verb 'they say' (sense not listed in the provided options). 'با داد' means 'with justice' (داد = justice) and 'شاپور کرد' states that Shapur 'did/established' it — i.e. 'they say Shapur established it with justice.'
segments.s553-g81: This phrase functions as an adverbial participial clause: به + کرده (past participle) yields a 'having done' sense, followed by the location بالای این پل. Word order is topical/poetic: the action phrase ('having made/placed') comes before the place phrase. بالای این پل = 'on top of this bridge.' هزار is the quantified object (a thousand [units/items]).
segments.s553-g82: بخواهی is a 2nd-person present/future form meaning 'you will want' or 'you may wish'. ز governs the source ('from the treasure'); آنچ is a relative pronoun 'that which' (i.e. whatever); آید here is impersonal/present 'comes' and combines with به کار (an idiom) meaning 'to come to use' or 'to be of use'. The whole clause says: 'you will want from the treasure that which comes to use.'
segments.s553-g83: تو is the addressed subject 'you.' از introduces the source: 'from (the) knowledge' — دانشی is the noun 'knowledge/learning' and is followed by the genitive modifier فیلسوفان روم 'the philosophers of Rome' (word order is noun + modifier in poetic register). The phrase means 'from the knowledge of Rome's philosophers.'
segments.s553-g84: فراز آر is an imperative-like construction: فراز 'on high/aloft' with آَر (here an imperative/causative of آراستن or 'bring/adorn') yields 'raise up' or 'lift up.' چَندی 'some/a little' is the amount to be raised. بران is a prepositional/demonstrative particle 'for that/on that,' and مرز و بوم is a fixed collocation meaning 'border and homeland' = 'realm/land.' Altogether: 'raise up some of it for this land.'
segments.s553-g85: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' این پل is the subject 'this bridge.' برآید is a present/subjunctive/impersonal verb 'rises/arrives/reaches' used here in a temporal sense: 'when the bridge reaches...' سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'; خان خویش (خوی + ش = 'his own') is the phrase 'his khan/lord/inn' (poetic ambiguity between lord and caravanserai).
segments.s553-g86: This is an imperative sequence: برو 'go!' followed by تازیان باش 'be (among) the Arabs' — تازیان is the group and باش is the imperative of بودن 'be.' مهمان خویش is a possessive phrase 'his guest' (مهمان = guest; خویش marked by the clitic ش is 'his/one's own'). The line instructs: 'go and be his guest among the Arabs.'
segments.s553-g87: This is a paired prepositional phrase giving manner: (ا)با شادمانی و با ایمنی = 'with joy and with safety.' The initial token 'ابا' is a poetic form here functioning as 'با' ('with'); شادمانی = 'rejoicing/joy' and ایمنی = 'security/safety.' Grammatically these are adverbial adjuncts modifying the implied action that follows.
segments.s553-g88: ز بد دور = 'far from evil' (ز = 'from'), and وز دست اهریمنی = 'and from the hand of the Ahrimanic one/demon.' Here وز is a fused poetic conjunction + preposition ('و ز' = 'and from'); دست is literally 'hand' (agent/agency), and اهریمنی is an adjective 'demonic/Ahrimanic.' The whole segment gives distance from harm: 'far from evil and from the demon's hand.'
segments.s553-g57: یکی is an indefinite introducer here (= “a certain”); it precedes the noun as is normal in Persian. شارستان (city) is the head noun, followed by نام + شاپور which is a postnominal name phrase “named Shapur.” گرد in this construction forms part of a verbal phrase with برآورد (an archaic poetic collocation): together they signal the action of erecting/raising the city.
segments.s553-g58: برآورد is a past/ perfective verbal root here meaning “brought forth/raised” or more loosely “built/established” (sense ‘accomplish/fulfil’). و simply coordinates the following verb. پرداخت in this context means “completed/finished” (an intransitive/ result verb). در روز is the usual prepositional time phrase “on the day (of)”; ارد here is an old proper name (a day/festival), used as a temporal noun.
segments.s553-g59: همی is a verbal particle used in older/poetic Persian to mark continuous or emphatic action (here giving an ongoing/imperfective flavor). سالار is the subject “the commander/chief.” زان شهر is a prepositional phrase “from/for that city.” رنج here is a noun “toil/hardship” and serves as the object or the burden the commander bears—word order is topical (object/oblique before the verb which follows in the next segment).
segments.s553-g60: بپردخت is a past-tense verb here meaning “he paid” or “he rendered/expended.” بسیار modifies the quantity (’much’ / ’a great deal’). با introduces an instrumental phrase “with” and رنج (here again ‘hardship/toil’) is the instrument/condition under which the action was done; گنج is the direct object “treasure/wealth.” The line’s word order (object and instruments grouped after the verb) is typical of older epic diction.
segments.s553-g61: یکی introduces “a certain/one” city. شارستان is the noun “city”; بود here is the past of بودن (“was”) describing existence/state. آباد is an adjective meaning “prosperous/inhabited” and بوم is a noun “land/realm” completing the description (literal order: ‘one city was a prosperous land’).
segments.s553-g62: بپردخت is again a past verb “he paid/provided.” بهر is a preposition meaning “for” or “on behalf of.” اسیران روم is the beneficiary phrase “the captives (prisoners) of Rome/Byzantium.” The clause as a whole says he furnished or set up the city for the Roman prisoners.
segments.s553-g63: در خوزیان is a locative/possessive frame “among the Khuzis / in Khuz (the Khuzis).” دارد is the present/persistent verb “has/holds” (stative possession). این بوم و بر is the noun phrase “this soil/land and domain/territory”; the demonstrative این precedes the nouns as usual.
segments.s553-g64: که introduces a relative clause “which/that.” دارند is plural present “they have/hold.” هرکس means “whoever / anyone.” بروبر is an adverbial or idiomatic compound meaning roughly “face-to-face / directly / exactly before one” (in older Persian often meaning ‘straight ahead’), and گذر is “passage/passing” or “passerby.” The clause says that whoever passes has (or finds) it directly — an idiomatic description of possession/visibility on passing.
segments.s553-g17: "کجا بود بر قیصران ارجمند" is a rhetorical question with inverted word order: کجا بود = "where was (there)" (past). بر قیصران ارجمند places the locus 'before/among the esteemed Caesars' (بر used in the classical sense of 'over/before/among' a group). The phrase as a whole asks whether such a person could be found in the presence of those great emperors.
segments.s553-g18: "کمند افگنی نامداری بلند" is a compact noun phrase describing the person sought: کمند افگنی = 'lasso‑throwing' (a compound meaning 'one who casts a snare/loop'), where افگنی is a deverbal/adjectival element indicating the agent/action. نامداری بلند stacks two modifiers 'renown' + 'lofty/high' (بلند intensifies the fame). The whole phrase therefore means 'a highly famed lasso‑thrower.'
segments.s553-g19: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; برخاست is the past 'arose/was raised'. آواز کوس is a genitive phrase 'the sound/voice of the war‑drum' and از دو روی = 'from two sides' (دو روی = both flanks/sides). The clause sets the scene: at the rising of the drums from both sides.
segments.s553-g20: ز قلب اندر آمد is literally 'from the heart came' (verb μετά position: آمد = 'came' with the adverbial phrase 'from the heart'). گو here functions as a quotative/introducer meaning 'saying' or an address particle 'O' before نامجوی; نامجوی is used as an appellation 'name‑seeker' (one who seeks fame). Together this gives 'out of the heart came the cry, "O seeker of a name!"'.
segments.s553-g21: وزین سو here functions as a locative/pointing phrase 'from that side/quarter' (سو = 'side'); بشد is the past/resultive 'it happened/there arose'. نامداری دلیر = 'renown of the brave' (نامداری = fame; دلیر qualifies it). The whole clause means 'from that side the brave's renown arose.'
segments.s553-g22: کجا here functions as the interrogative 'what/where (was)' in a rhetorical question; نام او بود = 'was his name' (او = 'he/his'), with بود as the past copula. گرزسپ شیر is given as the answer/appositive proper name ('Gorzesp‑Shir' — شیر here is an epithet 'lion'). The line reads as 'What was his name? Gorzesp‑Shir.'
segments.s553-g23: برآمد is 'arose/issued forth' (verb first for poetic effect). ز هر دو سپه = 'from both armies/hosts' (هر دو = both), and کوس و غو names what rose: 'drum and tumult' (kūs = war‑drum; غو = uproar). The clause describes the sound and commotion issuing from both sides.
segments.s553-g24: بجنبید here is a past/inchoative sense 'was moved/was stirred' (the heart was roused). در قلبگه = 'in the heart‑seat/bosom' and شاه نو = 'the new king' (شاه = king, نو = new). The clause therefore says the new king's heart was stirred in his breast.
segments.s553-g9: همی راند is a habitual/continuous past narrative: همی (an older/poetic particle) marks continued action, and راند is the past third-person singular of راندن 'to ride/drive'. تا + پیش + proper name gives the limit: تا 'until' + پیش 'before/in front of' + التوینه (a place or person) specifying where he rode up to.
segments.s553-g10: This is a nominal phrase describing what was encountered: سپاهی 'a host/troops' qualified by the adjective سبک 'light (armed)' placed after the noun, and the predicate-like phrase بی‌نیاز از بنه 'not needing / free of baggage' (بی‌نیاز + از + noun). بنه here is used concretely as a small bundle/baggage, so the phrase means 'self-sufficient, without baggage.'
segments.s553-g11: سپاهی is the subject 'a host/army'; ز (poetic form of از) means 'from'; قیدافه is a place/name; آمد برون is the verbal expression 'came forth' (آمد 'came' + برون 'out/forth'). Word order here is subject + prepositional origin + verb phrase: 'a host came out from Qeydafe.'
segments.s553-g12: که introduces a relative clause modifying the host ('which ...'). از گرد خورشید literally 'from/around the disk/circumference of the sun' (از + گرد + خورشید), and شد تیره‌گون is 'became dark-hued' (شد + adjective). The clause means they (the host) so filled the sky that they darkened the area around the sun.
segments.s553-g13: ز 'from' marks origin (poetic instead of از); التوینه is the place/name; هم‌چنین 'likewise/also' modifies the coming; لشکری is a collective noun 'an army/host' (لشکر + ی). The phrase means 'likewise a host came from Altuyna.'
segments.s553-g14: بیامد is the past 'came'. سپهدارشان is 'their commander' (سپهدار + pronominal suffix -شان). مهتری follows in apposition: either the commander's name or title ('Mahtari'/'the chief'). The verb precedes the nominal apposition here, a common narrative order: 'came — their commander, Mahtari.'
segments.s553-g15: برانوش is a proper name. بد نام is an adjectival phrase where بد 'bad/ill' qualifies نام 'name/reputation' (here 'ill-famed' or 'of ill repute'). آن پهلوان 'that champion' points back to the warrior just mentioned; the sequence places the name and its epithet alongside the noun 'champion.'
segments.s553-g16: سواری is a descriptive noun 'a rider/horseman' (here describing the champion). سرافراز and روشن‌روان are postpositive adjectives: سرافراز 'lofty/proud/honored' and روشن‌روان 'bright‑soul(ed)' i.e. 'cheerful/clear‑faced.' Persian commonly places such adjectives after the noun they modify.
segments.s553-g73: همی is the habitual/continuous adverb (literary) meaning 'continually' before the verb. برد here is the past stem of بردن 'to carry/take' used with همی to give an ongoing past sense; هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'in every direction' (هر quantifier + سو ‘side/toward’). برانوش را places the proper name Baranush as the definite object (را marks the object) of برد.
segments.s553-g74: بدو is the prepositional pronominal form 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb. داشتی is an instance of داشتن used idiomatically with گوش ('to have ear') meaning 'to pay attention' or 'to heed' (here treated as a main verb). در سخن is a prepositional phrase 'in speech/conversation' and گوش را is the noun 'ear/attention' marked by را as the definite object.
segments.s553-g75: یکی here introduces 'a certain' (indefinite) river. رود is the noun 'river' and is modified by the compound adjective بد پهن (literally 'bad/broad') where پهن 'wide' is the principal descriptor; در شوشتر places the river geographically 'in Shushtar.'
segments.s553-g76: که begins a relative/explanatory clause 'so that/which' describing the river. ماهی is the subject 'a fish'; نکردی is the negative past form (ن‑ + کردی) of کردن used impersonally/negatively here 'did not/could not (pass)'; بروبر is an adverb 'straight/face-to-face' modifying گذر, and گذر is the verbal-noun or noun 'pass/passage'—together conveying 'a fish did not pass straight across.'
segments.s553-g77: برانوش را places Baranush as the addressee/object (را marks the definite object). گفت‌گر is a nominal form 'the one who said' or 'the speaker' (here 'the geometrician who spoke'), and هندسی is an adjective 'geometric' describing that speaker — together: 'the geometrician said to Baranush.'
segments.s553-g78: پلی ساز is an imperative instruction 'build a bridge' where ساز functions as an old/implied imperative (equivalent to بساز). آنجا 'there' gives the location. چنانچون is a comparative conjunction 'such that/just as,' and رسی is a present/subjunctive form of رسیدن 'you may reach/arrive,' giving purpose/result: 'build a bridge there so that you may reach.'
segments.s553-g79: که here introduces a purposive clause 'so that / in order that.' ما بازگردیم is a first-person plural subjunctive 'we may return.' و links the two coordinated wishes/clauses, and آن پل به جای locates the bridge 'in its place' as the subject of the second conjunct.
segments.s553-g80: بماند is a subjunctive/optative form 'may remain' (wish/hope that it stay). به + دانایی indicates instrument/means 'by/through wisdom,' and رهنمای is the agent/noun 'guide' — together 'may it remain by the wisdom of a guide' (i.e., under wise guidance).
segments.s553-g41: که is the subordinating particle 'that', introducing the reported clause. چندین here is an adverbial/quantifier meaning 'many' or 'much' and precedes the subject for emphasis; تو is the emphatic/explicit 2nd-person subject. از بهر is a fixed causal phrase 'for the sake of / because of' and خون is the direct object 'blood' placed before the verb (typical Persian object–verb flexibility).
segments.s553-g42: بریزی is a 2nd-person present/future-form meaning 'you will spill / you spill' (classical/poetic present). The repeated pronoun تو is emphatic. با introduces the following noun phrase as an accompaniment or relation ('with / before / by'); داور here is an epithet 'the Judge' (contextually the divine judge) and رهنمون is an appositive epithet 'the Guide'. Together the prepositional phrase indicates being brought into the presence or judgment of the Judge.
segments.s553-g43: چه begins the interrogative 'what'. گویی here is intended as the verb 'you will say' (classical/poetic use), but the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense (so flagged below). چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause, پرسند is the verbal form 'they ask' (3rd plural present/classical), and روز شمار literally 'day-counter' = 'Day of Reckoning' or 'the judging day' (روز 'day' + شمار 'count/number').
segments.s553-g44: چه again 'what' (introducing the second question). پوزش is 'an apology / excuse' and کنی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative of 'to do/make' meaning 'will you make / shall you offer'. پیش means 'in the presence of / before (someone)' and پروردگار is 'the Lord' (God). Together: 'what excuse will you offer before the Lord?'.
segments.s553-g45: فرستیم is a 1st‑person-plural form used here hortatively/optatively ('let us send' or 'we will send') common in older Persian. باژی (with -ی ending) is the instrumental/object noun (baazh — here 'cry/message' or a named signal); چنان هم 'just so / in the same way' modifies the action; که introduces the relative clause and بود is the past form 'was' referring back to how it had been earlier.
segments.s553-g46: برین is a fused prepositional + demonstrative 'بر + این' meaning 'on/over this' or 'with respect to this'. نیز 'also' reinforces addition. دردی is 'a pain/trouble' (object), and نباید + infinitive (فزود here) expresses an impersonal prohibition 'one must not / it should not be that ... increases' — i.e. 'no additional pain should be added.' فزود is the verb 'to increase' used in the infinitival sense following نباید.
segments.s553-g47: همان 'the same' (i.e. the same measure/response) and نیز 'also/likewise' set the parallel action. با + باژ indicates the instrument or accompaniment 'with Baazh' (Baazh being the means or name of the messenger/cry). فرمان کنیم is the hortative/first‑person plural construction 'let us order / let us issue a command' (کنیم being the subjunctive/optative form of 'to do/make').
segments.s553-g48: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from / among'. خویشان فراوان = 'many relatives' (خویشان 'relatives', فراوان 'plentiful / many') and گروگان کنیم uses کنیم ('let us take / make') with گروگان 'hostage' as object: 'let us take many relatives as hostages.' Word order places the source group before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s553-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. التوینه is a place-name (proper noun) here. بازگردی is the infinitival/nominalized verb 'to return' (the act of returning) and is the thing being judged by رواست. رواست is روا + ست (the adjective 'permissible/fitting' plus the copula 'is'), so the whole clause says 'turning back from Altūyneh is permissible.'
segments.s553-g50: فرستیم is a first-person-plural verb form expressing deliberate action ('we will send' / 'let us send') typical of epic style. با is the preposition 'with' and باژ here names the manner — 'with a shout/cry'. هرچت is هرچ 'whatever' with the enclitic -ت 'your' (= 'whatever you...'), and هواست is هوا + ست 'is the desire/mood', so the clause means 'we will send with a shout whatever you wish.'
segments.s553-g51: همی بود is the poetic imperfect/past-continuous 'was / remained' (همی giving continuous aspect). شاپور is the subject. تا here means 'as far as' or 'even to', so تا باژ و ساو links Shapur's staying/movement to the places that follow (باژ and ساو). The two place-names are coordinated by و.
segments.s553-g52: فرستاد is simple past 'sent'. قیصر follows the verb in an inverted poetic order and is the agent 'the Caesar'. ده is the numeral 'ten', انبان is 'sacks/pouches', and گاو is 'cattle/cows' — together 'ten sacks of cattle' (i.e. ten loads of cattle) as the thing sent.
segments.s553-g53: غلام و پرستار is a coordinated noun phrase 'slave(s) and attendant(s)'. رومی follows and qualifies them ('Roman' / from Rûm). هزار (positioned after the phrase) is the numeral 'a thousand' indicating the quantity of these people.
segments.s553-g54: گرانمایه دیبا is a noun phrase 'precious brocade(s)'. The particle/word نه here (appearing in the text) is the numeral 'nine' modifying the brocades; اندر شمار is a prepositional phrase 'in the count' or 'included in the tally' — i.e. 'nine precious brocades counted among them.'
segments.s553-g55: بالتوینه is a place-name (locative); در is the preposition 'in/at'. ببد is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'it was / it became' (3rd sg.), and روز هفت ('the seventh day') is the temporal predicate — so the clause says 'At Baltūyneh it was the seventh day.'
segments.s553-g56: ز روم is the prepositional phrase 'from Rûm' (Byzantium). اندر آمد is the verbal phrase 'entered / came in', and به اهواز رفت is 'went to Ahvâz' — two past actions presented in sequence: he came from Rûm and then went to Ahvâz.
segments.s553-g89: به تدبیر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by the plan/through the design' (به + noun). آن پل is a noun phrase 'that bridge' (آن as demonstrative before the noun here). باستاد is an adjectival/qualifying element meaning 'masterly/skillful' modifying مرد ('man'); word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (qualifier placed before the noun it qualifies).
segments.s553-g90: فراز is an adverb/adverbial noun 'up/on high' and modifies the verbal phrase that follows. آوریدش is past (3rd person plural) 'they brought him/it' with the clitic -ش (him/it) attached. بران is a locative/adverbial 'thereon/onto that place' linking to فراز; کارکرد here functions as the verbal idea '(they) did the work / set him to the work' (classic Persian often writes کار کرد together in poetry).
segments.s553-g91: بپردخت is the past tense 'he paid / dispensed' (بپرداخت). شاپور is the subject name. گنجی is the direct object 'treasure' (here used as what Shapur spent). بران is an adverbial 'for that / on that' linking this expenditure to the preceding matter.
segments.s553-g92: که introduces a subordinate purposive clause 'so that / in order that'. زان literally 'from that' and refers back to the foregoing (i.e. 'by/for that reason'). باشد is the optative/subjunctive 'may be / should be' (classical Persian use to express purpose/result). آسانی مردمان = 'the ease/convenience of the people' (آسانی = ease; مردمان = people).
segments.s553-g93: چو شد is a temporal clause 'when (it) became / when ... happened' (چو = 'when', شد = past 'became/was'). شه here functions as 'ruler/king' (used in poetry; titles can be applied to a female ruler as well). برانوش is a personal name (the subject in this clause); کرد آن تمام = 'she did that completely / she finished it' (کرد = past 'did', آن تمام = 'that in its entirety'). Note the inverted order typical of epic poetry.
segments.s553-g94: پلی here is a poetic/dialectal verbal or verbalized form related to 'پل/پریدن/گام برداشتن' and is used with کرد as a light verb to express the action 'stepped / traversed'. کرد is the past 'did' forming the compound verbal expression. بالا is an adverb 'upwards / on high'. هزارانش is 'its thousands' (هزاران + possessive -ش), and گام = 'steps' — together 'upwards by thousands of steps' (i.e. she ascended in many steps).
segments.s553-g95: چو شد پل تمام = 'when the bridge became finished' (چو شد = 'when it became', پل تمام = 'the bridge complete'). او is the subject 'he/she'. ز ششتر برفت is a classical idiom 'from the shashtar (lute) he departed / he put the lute aside' (ز = 'from', ششتر = a lute/instrument; برفت is past of رفتن with the prefix بر- indicating departure/removal). In context this means he ceased playing and laid aside the instrument.
segments.s553-g96: سوی = 'toward(s)'. خان خود = 'his khan' (خان can mean lord, chief, or an inn/palace; here contextually 'his lord/house/seat'). روی بنهاد is the compound verb 'to set/lay the face toward' = 'to turn (one's) face toward / to face' (روی = 'face', بنهاد = past of بنهادن 'to put/place'). تفت is a short word here whose meaning in this line is unclear/archaic; it may be emphatic or adverbial but does not match the provided glosses directly.
segments.s553-g1: وزان پس is a temporal-prepositional phrase placed at the clause front meaning 'from that time/onward'; پراگنده شد is a compound verb meaning 'became scattered/ spread' in past tense; آگهی is the thing that spread (the 'news' or 'announcement') and, in this poetic word order, follows the verb rather than preceding it.
segments.s553-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'; بیکار شد is a resultative past 'became empty/vacant' with بیکار as predicate-adjective and شد as the past verb; تخت شاهنشهی ('the imperial throne') is the logical subject which follows the verb here because of poetic inversion.
segments.s553-g3: بمرد is an archaic/poetic past-form verb meaning 'died' (here used as the narrative past); it stands before the name for emphasis. 'اردشیر آن خردمند شاه' is a sequence of name + demonstrative/article + epithet + title in apposition ('Ardashir, that wise king').
segments.s553-g4: به شاپور is an indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to Shapur'; بسپرد is the past-tense verb 'he entrusted/handed over' (3rd person); گنج و سپاه are the direct objects 'treasure and army' following the verb, joined by و.
segments.s553-g5: خروشی (a noun 'a roar/clamor') is the event that 'برآمد' ('arose/issued forth') — past tense verb. ز هر مرز و بوم is a prepositional phrase 'from every border and land' specifying the source; the order (noun + verb + prepositional phrase) reflects poetic flexibility.
segments.s553-g6: ز قیدافه marks the origin/source 'from Qeydafe' (Qeydafe is an obscure proper name/place); برداشتند is a past plural verb 'they raised/lifted' or 'they took up'; باژ روم is the direct object meaning 'the cry "Rome"' with باژ 'cry' and روم naming the content of that cry.
segments.s553-g7: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause 'when ... came/arrived'; آگاهی (information/news) is the subject that آمد ('came/arrived') and به شاپور شاه is the recipient phrase 'to Shapur the king' (به marking the indirect object).
segments.s553-g8: بیاراست is a past third-person singular verb meaning 'he arrayed/ made ready / adorned'; its subject is the king implied from the previous clause (Shapur). 'کوس و درفش و سپاه' are the coordinated direct objects 'drum and standard and army' that he readied.
segments.s553-g25: This is a causal prepositional phrase headed by the preposition ز meaning 'because of' (classical poetic use). بس is a quantifying modifier ('so much' / 'many'), and ناله governs the following coordinated instruments (بوق و هندی ...). The whole chunk functions as the reason/cause for the following verb phrase.
segments.s553-g26: همی is the archaic/prosodic particle that often marks ongoing or vivid narrative action (here giving a continuous/iterative flavor). چرخ و ماه‌اندر is the coordinated subject ('the heavens and the moon'); آمد is a past narrative verb 'came/moved'; آمد ز جای is a light, idiomatic phrase 'moved from their place' where ز + جای indicates origin/location.
segments.s553-g27: ببستند is a past transitive verb 'they bound/tied' (3rd plural). تبیره is the direct object (an item fastened) and بر پشت پیل is a prepositional locative 'on the elephant's back' (بر + noun). Word order is object – verb – locative in classical narrative style.
segments.s553-g28: This clause contains a compound verbal idea: همی‌بر شد where همی gives iterative/continuative force and شد is the past auxiliary/lexical 'became / was carried', so the sense is 'was carried/rose'. آوازشان is the possessed noun 'their cry/voice' (آواز + -شان). بر دو میل here expresses extent/distance ('for/to two miles'); modern equivalents would use بر + distance.
segments.s553-g29: This clause strings several predicates: زمین is the subject; جنب جنبان شد is a reduplicated verb phrase built on the root جنب (to move) with an intensive form جنبان/جنبان شدن meaning 'to shake/tremble; to move violently' and شد as the past auxiliary 'became'. و links the next coordinated clause. پر ز گرد is a predicative phrase 'full of dust' where پر + ز + noun means 'filled with'.
segments.s553-g30: چو is a simile/comparative particle 'like/as'; it introduces the comparison 'like fire'. آتش درخشان is a noun phrase 'shining fire' modifying سنان 'spears'. نبرد here functions as a noun ('battle') or a nominalized verb; syntactically the line compresses into a vivid image: the spears, shining like fire, were in battle / made battle.
segments.s553-g31: This is a rhetorical question: روانی (mental/state of mind) is the topic; کجا 'where' asks for the location/existence; با خرد بود جفت uses با + noun ('with wisdom') and بود جفت 'was paired' (بود as past copula + جفت 'pair/companion'). Word order is emphatic classical Persian: topic – interrogative – predicate.
segments.s553-g32: ستاره is the subject 'star'; همی again gives iterative/continuous vividness; the expected verb 'باریدن' (to fall/rain) is here represented by a form that in the provided lexicon is absent, so the line means 'a star kept falling from the sky'. از چرخ 'from the sky/heaven' is a simple prepositional source; گفت at the end (literally 'said') frames this as a reported/rumored statement.

sentences.s553-s17: Baranush, a warrior, was seized in her inner heart, with a heart full of blood.
sentences.s553-s18: And from them three thousand Romans were killed, (lying) in the battle line at Baltuyna.
sentences.s553-s19: One thousand two hundred were captured, and the warriors’ hearts became full of concern.
sentences.s553-s20: The Caesar sent a certain learner (envoy) to the presence of Shapur, King Ardashir.
sentences.s553-s33: In Pars he raised an inner lofty town, clean and useful.
sentences.s553-s34: A certain man built a town in Sistan, in that place of many date-groves.
sentences.s553-s35: One half of it Ardashir had made; the other half (by) Shapur Gird, bold and brave.
sentences.s553-s36: He made the old fortress into the city of Nishapur; they say Shapur established it with justice.
sentences.s553-s41: Having placed a thousand upon the top of this bridge, you will take from the treasure whatever proves useful.
sentences.s553-s42: You, draw from the learning of Rome’s philosophers and raise up some of it for this borderland.
sentences.s553-s43: When this bridge reaches his khan, go — among the Arabs be his guest.
sentences.s553-s44: With rejoicing and with safety, far from evil and from the hand of the demon.
sentences.s553-s29: He built a city called Shapur and completed it on the day of Ard.
sentences.s553-s30: The commander bore the toils for that city; he paid out great treasure with much hardship.
sentences.s553-s31: There was a city — a prosperous dwelling — which he provided for the Roman captives.
sentences.s553-s32: This land and territory belongs to the Khuzis, and whoever passes by holds it directly/in full view.
sentences.s553-s9: Where among the noble Caesars was there a famed, lofty lasso‑thrower?
sentences.s553-s10: When the sound of the war‑drum rose from both sides, out of the heart there came the cry, 'O seeker of a name!'
sentences.s553-s11: From that quarter there rose the brave renown; what was his name? Gorzesp‑Shir.
sentences.s553-s12: From both hosts the drums and clamor rose, and in the king's breast the new sovereign was stirred.
sentences.s553-s5: He kept riding until he came before Altūyneh — a light-armed host, self-sufficient and without baggage.
sentences.s553-s6: A host came out from Qeydafe, which became dark-hued around the sun.
sentences.s553-s7: From Altuyna likewise a host came; their commander was Mahtari.
sentences.s553-s8: That champion, Brānush — ill-famed — a proud rider and cheerful in spirit.
sentences.s553-s37: He was continually carrying Baranush off in every direction; he lent him an ear in speech.
sentences.s553-s38: A certain wide (and rough) river in Shushtar, so wide that a fish could not pass straight across.
sentences.s553-s39: The geometrician said to Baranush, 'Build a bridge there so that you may reach [the other side].'
sentences.s553-s40: so that we may return and that the bridge remain in its place under the guidance of wisdom.
sentences.s553-s21: That you will shed much blood for dinars and be brought before the Judge, the Guide.
sentences.s553-s22: What will you say when the Day of Reckoning asks? What excuse will you make before the Lord?
sentences.s553-s23: Let us send the same baazh as before; no further suffering should be added to this.
sentences.s553-s24: Likewise, let us issue the same command with Baazh and take many relatives as hostages.
sentences.s553-s25: It is permissible to turn back from Altūyneh; we will send (it) with a shout — whatever you desire.
sentences.s553-s26: Shapur remained even as far as Bāž and Sāv; the Caesar sent ten sacks of cattle.
sentences.s553-s27: A thousand Roman slaves and attendants, and nine precious brocades in the count.
sentences.s553-s28: At Baltūyneh it became the seventh day; he came from Rûm and went to Ahvâz.
sentences.s553-s45: By the contrivance of that bridge a skilled man stood there; they brought him up on high and set him to that task.
sentences.s553-s46: Shapur paid out treasure for that, so that there might be ease for the people.
sentences.s553-s47: When Baranush became ruler, she finished that; she climbed the bridge, making thousands of steps.
sentences.s553-s48: When the bridge was finished, he put aside the lute and turned his face toward his khan (home/master) — (tft).
sentences.s553-s1: From that time news spread that the imperial throne had become vacant.
sentences.s553-s2: Ardashir, that wise king, died; he entrusted the treasure and the army to Shapur.
sentences.s553-s3: A shout arose from every border and land; from Qeydafe they raised the cry 'Rome'.
sentences.s553-s4: When the news reached King Shapur, he arrayed the drum, the banner, and the army.
sentences.s553-s13: Because of so many cries of trumpet and Indian drum, the sky and the moon moved from their places.
sentences.s553-s14: They bound a covering/harness on the elephant's back; their cry carried for two miles.
sentences.s553-s15: The earth quivered and shook, and became full of dust; like fire, the shining spears (were) in battle.
sentences.s553-s16: Where, then, was a sane mind paired with wisdom? They say a star kept falling from the sky.
